reviews.review_text:
[h1] THE GOLDEN AGE OF EMPIRE BAY [/h1]

[b][i]  45 hours walking the snowy and sun-drenched streets of Empire Bay. Vito Scaletta's journey is the ultimate definition of what it means to be a "Wise Guy." The brotherhood between Vito and Joe is easily one of the greatest bonds in gaming history.

The music on the radio, the gritty winter atmosphere, and a story full of betrayal and sacrifices... This game is a timeless classic that perfectly captures the cost of ambition.

As far back as I can remember, I always wanted to be a gangster. 9.5/10  [/i][/b]

One of my all time favourite games and the best game in the Mafia series (So far) Mafia II has aged like a fine wine and the definitive edition runs and plays smooth as butter. Extremely quotable voice acting work throughout and with the original version releasing in 2010 its crazy the level and attention to detail for many things you can interact with in the games environment that put a lot of other open world modern releases to shame. Highly recommend for new fans of the series!

"look how they massacred my boy"

How this has positive reviews is beyond me. This game is full of bugs, including a game breaking bug at the end of the game that can prevent you from finishing, requiring you to restart the longest chapter in the game. Ground textures flash, characters look retarded during cutscenes, texture draw distance is worse than the original, road signs will randomly blur or flash. 

None of the original bugs were fixed and several new ones were introduced.

Mafia 2 had what was considered revolutionary eye tracking during cutscenes, characters were always looking exactly where they were supposed to and followed NPCs around as they talked. In the DE version they are often cross-eyed, rolling around like they're at a rave, or staring off into empty space like an invalid, completely ruining the cinematography Mafia 2 is known for. Serious cutscenes look goofy as ♥♥♥♥.

It's egregious, this is one of the greatest games from my youth and 2k did not do it justice. By chapter 6 I regretted not playing the original because of all the visual glitches, game breaking bugs aside (and yet to be discovered). This is an absolute turd of a "definitive edition"

♥♥♥♥ you 2k, you own a brilliant IP and treat it like ♥♥♥♥.

[h2]Base game - 3.5/5[/h2]
[h2]DLCs – 2/5[/h2]

It’s easy to fall for Mafia 2. Especially now, after the release of the remake of the original game, because it shows how ahead it was. Keep in mind that this is just a remaster, while Mafia: Definitive Edition is a full remake of the original Mafia.

BUT, first things first. I must mention that this Definitive Edition is very buggy. It will often crash and many cutscenes are a visual mess, with deformed/missing models and messed up textures. My personal advice is to download “Mafia 2 DE - Enhanced Edition” mod from Nexus and then SilentPatch, just in case, to not have issues with the savegames (after installing SilentPatch you MUST start a new game, you can’t load your older saves). There are many other issues you might encounter depending on your machine, so I suggest that you check out PCGamingWiki section for Mafia 2: Definitive Edition, as they list many fixes there.

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3734653239

Mafia 2 is still a great experience. The story almost mirrors the story of the first game, and even though I’m a bigger fan of Tommy Angelo, personally, it’s hard not to love Vito and Joe, they’re pretty much the icons of the series. Both stories follow ordinary men struggling to get by until they begin taking small jobs for the mafia, eventually rising within the family. Vito is a bit different compared to Tommy though. His character feels a bit flat. Tommy admired the respect regular folks had towards Salieri, and he wanted that. Vito wanted the flashy life. He loved the fearsome respect, he loved the money, fast sports cars, flashy suits and expensive escorts (the collectibles being the Playboy magazines are so out of pocket, but it’s still absolutely iconic). Him and Joe were ready to take any job available in order to get that life, no questions asked. Tommy did what he did because he thought it was the best way to provide for his family long-term, his wife and children. Vito did it because he was power hungry. He deeply refused to end up like his father, a hard-working, yet impoverished man that was abused by his boss to the point that it cost him his life. Both of these protagonists ultimately pay the price for their actions, but Tommy leaves a legacy that loves him and remembers him, while Vito has absolutely nobody. [spoiler]His mother died while he was in prison, carrying his promise that he never fulfilled, his sister abandoned him because of his actions, and his best buddy Joe probably pays with his life (that's the cliffhanger) because they wanted the fame for themselves.[/spoiler]
Even though I really liked the story of Mafia 2, it feels underdeveloped or rushed at certain parts. The prison part could have been better. It pretty much only serves as an introduction to the game’s combat mechanics and as a link to Leo Galante for the story plot. And the ending is a massive, massive let-down. In the original Mafia, the redemption arc, the greater purpose, is what made the story finale so iconic. In Mafia 2, Vito did what he had to do, simply in order to save his a*s. For him, it was just another hit. But the biggest what-if in the story of Mafia 2 was the story of Henry, which is more than worthy of a DLC. It could have been an interesting retrospect from a completely different point of view.

When it comes to mechanics, it is very, very close to the new remake of the original Mafia. It’s a cover-based shooter with a simple hand-to-hand combat system. The shooting part is a bit iffy and it’s not as realistic as in the remake, but the close combat system is better here, even though it’s still very plain and simple. The vehicle handling is also pretty much the same, that is if you use the “simulation” mode in Mafia 2. Personally, that should have been the default, because the current default handling mode is very simple, resembling that of Grand Theft Auto series. The car customization is decently fun. The variation of weapons here is okay, as it was one of the things I didn’t like about the new remake. You can also hold all of these weapons with you, so the selection is very big at all times.

Empire Bay, based mostly on New York, looks very good. The snowy opening part of the game still looks and feels absolutely majestical, and the atmosphere is just phenomenal. The open-world of Mafia 2 is rich in detail, however I would still say that Lost Heaven in Mafia: Definitive Edition looks better. There isn’t much to do after the game is completed, because there are no side missions in Mafia 2, the story is linear and it consists of chapters, but there is a notable amount of things to do while roaming. I’ve really liked some of the details, like the NPCs slipping on ice, and hubcaps falling off of wheels when you drive too fast or slam the car after a jump. I also really liked the opening part when Vito comes back to his old neighborhood, and the NPCs recognize him and greet him. The sad part is that this is the only time something like this happens.

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3734434632

The police system has some pros and cons compared to Mafia: Definitive Edition. Most notably, you’re rarely punished for speeding. I’ve never even used the limiter in Mafia 2, while in Mafia: Definitive Edition you’re guaranteed to attract police attention if you’re driving over the speed limit. Even when you do get into a police chase, you’re escaping them within a few seconds. The police AI is decent and sometimes it can be tricky to escape them when you’re wanted. They don’t immediately know when the crime is committed, so you’re not “pinpointed” like you are in GTA games. I like the simple concept of being wanted and evading the police until you change your clothes or your license plates.

The Definitive Edition doesn’t improve much on the original graphics in terms of textures, but the shadows and lighting overall look much better. The original soundtrack is there, and man... There are 4 radio stations only, with one being the Press Radio News, but the music choice for the others is phenomenal. Empire Classic Radio and Empire Central Radio with big band, swing and pop classics from the 1940s and 1950s, and the fantastic Delta Radio with rock-and-roll, jazz and blues tracks from the 1950s that are pretty much cult hits and relevant to this day. 

Even if your PC is considered high-end by today’s standards, you might experience MASSIVE FPS drops while in intense shootouts with a lot of environmental destruction, due to the usage of NVIDIA PhysX. It’s an old physics engine and pretty much abandoned in modern gaming, so the driver support for newer RTX graphics is not the best when it comes to many of the older titles.

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3734625031

For the final mention, the DLCs. I’ve played them and I absolutely do not recommend you playing them. They add absolutely nothing to the story, and pretty much all of the missions are the exact same. These timed missions exist purely to add a ranking system where you earn points for certain actions and bonus points for faster completion times, which then determine your rank. They are unrelated to the events of the main story and add absolutely no context whatsoever. If you’re really keen on trying them, I just suggest you to play Joe’s Adventure, or even better, check the opening and closing missions on a YouTube playthrough. It will save you a bunch of time.

Mafia 2 is still worth playing, even though it didn’t get the treatment we all hoped for with the Definitive Edition. Hopefully, one day we’ll get a remake as respective as the first one.
I really don't see the point of this remaster. All the saves from the classic Mafia 2 still work, the graphics changes are barely noticeable, and it's honestly better to just play the original.

🚗 🏙️ Mafia II: Definitive Edition – let's be entirely honest, the main game is an absolute banger. The story of Vito Scalletta is peak narrative design, and his dynamic with Joe Barbaro is nothing short of legendary. When you're just playing through Empire Bay with those two, the game completely hooks you.

But man, the "Definitive Edition" label is a bit of a stretch. The graphics are still kinda ♥♥♥♥♥♥, and the remaster left a lot to be desired. And don't even get me started on the DLCs—they are absolute trash. Whether it’s Joe’s Adventures or the Jimmy expansions, the storytelling completely falls apart. Instead of the deep narrative from the main game, you're just mindlessly driving from point A to point B to kill a few generic enemies over and over again.

If you're an achievement hunter, this game will absolutely break you. Who in their right mind thought forcing players to drive 1,000 ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ miles in a DLC for a single achievement was a good idea? Combine that with hunting down 189 hidden wanted posters, and you have to ask yourself: are we deadass right now?

Personally, Mafia I: Definitive Edition completely clears this one. Tommy Angelo just edges out Vito as a protagonist for me—though if I swung that way, I'd crush on Vito in a heartbeat because the man is undeniably smooth. It's an incredible experience, but living in the shadow of giants like the GTA series makes it tough for a masterpiece like this to hold onto the massive, mainstream fanbase it deserves.

🟢 A gripping, cinematic main story with Vito and Joe that stands the test of time
🔴 Lazy, repetitive DLCs that completely strip away what makes the main game great
🚗 A soul-crushing achievement grind that will test your absolute sanity

9/10 – "An absolute masterpiece of a story, slightly held back by a lazy remaster."
Wish I played mafia 2 alot sooner im not sure if the original had controller sensitivity setting but this one does not, makes the gamer harder than it actually is, still fun
Best game out of the other three, goated soundtrack, peak story.
Worse than Mafia I, but still good. The story is very linear and on the short side, but it makes it a good game for a long week-end.

Unlike Mafia 1, I feel the 'Definitive Edition' label is valid here, it does indeed feel like a HD version of the OG game that hasn't been messed with and I appreciate that. So yeah, get this game, Mafia II is great.

Now, the negative. The DLC has to be some of the worst I've ever seen, I'm not even going to bother detailing why, just don't bother. There's nothing story related there, it's just make-work tasks and a small amount of cut content. It's not a problem since it's free so just don't play it. If you really want to put up with it for the cut content, just play Joe's Adventures, but don't expect much. Hard difficulty in the DLC gets to be overly frustrating unlike the base game, but it's not necessary for anything achievement-wise.

If you are silly like me and seeking all the achievements, good news! It's not broken like Mafia 3 DE. Bad news - there are 3 horrible achievements, and a bunch for the DLC. Fortunately you can skip playing one of the DLCs, but sadly you will still spend more time in the DLC than in the base game, and grind for hours after completing every other task.
i wanna say its a good game but there are way too many bugs, not to mention the amount of times it crashed.

The story is awesome, driving and shooting feels realistic. The soundtrack is amazing aswell. had fun with the campaign from beginning to end. The only thing that really sucked was the boring and unnecessary DLC. Without the DLC it's a 10/10 otherwise a 8/10.

Fine, but definitely not any different from the original game, just graphically "added into". If you feel like the previous game's engine that's meant for older operating systems is weighing you down, then this version is for you as it has all of the DLCs included anyways.

It had some glitches I didn’t experience in the original, but it’s still a well-done remaster. The improved visuals are nice, and the core experience remains just as enjoyable. I really didn’t need the “extra gift” of like 30 more collectibles… thanks, I suppose.
My absolutely favorite game. If you want to experience old New York, authentic cars, music, this game is for you!

Mafia 2 sold 5 million copies across its lifetime and what better game to make a remaster for than this one right here. out of the 3 games THIS ONE is the best. It's almost like getting buck ass wild with a silverfish and letting shawty fart without having a care in the world. do ME the favor and buy this and love it. 

8/10

Absolutely phenomenal! Games like this are not made much today. Such a great story until the end. 

The def edition can be buggy at times, but nothing to serious to tamper with your overall experience.

Mafia II is my favourite entry in the Mafia Franchise, just somewhat beating out Mafia 1 DE. However, the Definitive Edition of Mafia II is complete garbage, it's all still there the original foundations of Mafia 2, the writing, voice acting, atmosphere, soundtrack, the story, its all there and feels right, in a game world that is riddled with bugs.

[h1]The Story, Characters, and World[/h1]
Vit's story remains just as engaging and entertaining as it always has been. At his core Vito is a man who is defined by what he saw in his father, the man he refuses to become. During childhood having watched his father drink himself and their family into further poverty, which never left him even into adulthood seeing his father as a "deadbeat". His personal drive isn't like the traditional mafia story, he doesn't desire power or control (like joe's dream to be a capo), his desire is far more personal being quite materialistic and monetary, simply being a promise to himself to "never end up like his old man". Whether its an ambition or learned survival skill this desire drives everything he does in the game, willing to do whatever it takes to earn money to have a better life for himself.

Notably, Vito never chooses to settle down or start a family of his own, whether that's a consequence to his Mafia lifestyle, or by choice that he doesn't want him having many close to him that could be targeted. It is also possible that Vito's avoidance to a quiet family life is an unconscious decision to avoid the domestic pressures he grew up watching with his parents, witness his father drink himself to what for years he thought was his own drowning death, until it is later revealed to have been darker than that.
His surrogate father figure in Leo is one of the more underrated dynamics in the game. Leo is stubborn, old-world, stiff in the way only a man who has survived that long in that world can be. And yet there is something quietly caring there, at least as Vito sees it. Leo telling Vito not to stick his neck out for him reads less as dismissal and more as a kind of gruff protectiveness or the closest to warmth that someone like Leo knows how to show. However, it's also worth noticing that Leo has every reason to value Vito beyond pure emotional sentiment, or loyalty. [spoiler]Vito makes Leo rich from all the fights in the prison chapter, understanding and utilising his capabilities to place bets.[/spoiler] that profitability could also be why [spoiler]Leo even saves Vito in the end[/spoiler] rather than seeing VIto as a son he could understand Vito's worth to the organisation.

[h1]Vito and Joe[/h1]
If Leo represents the father Vito never really had, then Joe is the brother he chose. Their friendship feels effortless and lived-in from the very first scene, the banter, the loyalty, the way they drag each other into increasingly catastrophic situations without a second thought. Joe is in many ways Vito's opposite. Where Vito is guarded, pragmatic, and driven by a quiet personal fear of poverty, Joe is loud, impulsive, and genuinely hungry for status, his dream of becoming a capo isn't just ambition, it's the identity he desires most. And yet they work, and they protect each other, because underneath all of it Joe would do anything for Vito and Vito would do anything for Joe.

Which is what makes the ending land the way it does.

[spoiler]Vito doesn't go to the planetarium to save Joe. Joe wasn't supposed to be there. Vito tears through Falcone's men to survive it's only when he gets inside and is surprised when Joe stands there next to him, gun pointed at him on Falcone's orders, that the situation becomes something else entirely. Joe turns on Falcone instead, and together they finish it, a true demonstration of their friendship; Joe's reason, Vito owes him five "bucks" an amusing comedic last comment for the duo...

What happens next is quiet, almost mundane in how casual the betrayal is. They walk out. Leo's men are waiting. Joe is guided toward a separate car with a vague promise of a cathouse celebration, and Vito watches him go. Leo's final words of "sorry kid, but he wasn't part of the deal". Vito's expression in that moment says everything. He didn't anticipate it and he clearly isn't at peace with it. [/spoiler] If only we could of gotten the cut content mission, cheers to the modding community.
 
[h1]The Soundtrack[/h1]
The licensed soundtrack is doing enormous heavy lifting throughout. Hearing period-accurate radio stations shift from big band and early rock and roll as the years progress is one of those details that sounds minor until you're driving through a snowstorm at night and the music makes you feel genuinely embedded in that era. It is the kind of atmospheric craft that money can replicate but care has to underpin, and the original Mafia 2 had that care in abundance. I found the Christmas music to be especially nostalgic.

The setting of Empire Bay is especially captivating, this new york esc city. Not a sandbox entirely, but just enough little activities, robbing shops, customising and upgrading your cars, buying clothes, or playing the DLC additional missions there is lots to explore. It feels and fits perfectly into its 1940s - 1950s vibes.

For the story alone, I would recommend Mafia II but I would recommend the [b]classic[/b] game and not the definitive buggy mess and on easy or normal difficulty because hard, is once again not difficult but gruelling agony in the latter missions, unnecessarily, even for thematic sense of who Vito is going against.

---

My game keeps getting stuck forever at the loading scene after a shoot out inside a building in the game like what the heck I need help not refund I really want to continue this story. But other than than that this game is for the 9 hours I played it is a lot of fun! I'm really like Mafia series

I wish we could review the DLC seperately because it absolutely ♥♥♥♥♥ the bed compared to the main game...

Main game is great. If anything it feels a little jarring to play it after Mafia: Definitive Edition as the first entry is a remake of the original. But you've got all the familiar gameplay mechanics, immersion and experiences you'd expect from the first game. 

The DLC is terrible, absolutely terrible. Suddenly the gameplay becames very arcade with combos, arbitrary timers and scoring. The checkpoint system totally ruins it requiring you to repeat hours of gametime when you fail. And since enemies just spawn out of thin air and suddenly have x-ray vision, you will fail.

eh prob like a 3/5. Wish there was less driving around and more gunplay. 70% of the game is taxi driver simulator. The story is also kind of weird as you don't really feel like part of the mafia but more of an outsider.
Mafia I Definitive Edition Story and Graphics are better. Therefore this is a good game if you never played the original before.

I enjoyed this game quite a lot! It had a lot of the improvements I had hoped for from Mafia 1 that made the gameplay more like GTA, which gives the player more control about how long you want to run around the map and do basically what you want. Wanted status relied on the car description and outfit description, have to clear both to get back to real business. Really fun collectables!

A few issues that made this game less than perfect: 
1: 2k login popup every time the game boots, but it's skippable.
2: Controller setup. There are 2, 1 is garbage and the other is tolerable.
3: I got a weird dialog repeat glitch starting around chapter 14 [?]. It wasn't every cut scene or conversation, but it meant turning off the volume until the talking ended.
4: Early in the game I started noticing a visual stutter or hitching that got worse as the gameplay continued. Turning off threaded optimization in the nvidia menu fixed that immediately.

I've played through Mafia 2 multiple times in the past and still think it's an incredibly good game. After playing the Mafia 1 remake, I revisited this version and realized that this title desperately needs a full remake in the exact style of the first one.

[b]The Pros:[/b]
[list]
[*][b]Story & Dynamic Duo:[/b] The narrative surrounding Vito Scaletta and his best buddy Joe Barbaro is the absolute highlight of the game. It completely grips you from the very beginning until the credits roll.
[*][b]Masterpiece Soundtrack:[/b] The music is on a whole different level. The soundtrack is so brilliant that I still find myself listening to it outside of the game.
[*][b]Joe's Adventures DLC:[/b] The DLC centered around Joe is fantastic. It's a great addition that gives more screen time to one of the best characters in the game.
[/list]

[b]The Cons:[/b]
[list]
[*][b]Performance Issues (Stuttering):[/b] For a "Definitive Edition," you would expect solid performance. Unfortunately, the game stutters quite a bit, especially while driving around the city.
[*][b]Slow Pacing:[/b] Certain missions feel way too slow. On repeat playthroughs, there are several sequences that you just wish you could skip entirely.
[*][b]Terrible Checkpoints:[/b] The checkpoint system is poorly designed. If you take a break and close the game, you get thrown back pretty far. I highly recommend always finishing the entire chapter before quitting.
[/list]

[b]Verdict:[/b]
If you can overlook the performance hiccups and the outdated look, the game is still absolutely worth it for the story alone. However, since a proper remake is already in the works, it might be wiser to just wait for that instead or buy it at a sale.

Mafia II, though the definitive edition is full of bugs and graphical glitches, 
still, still, the story is worth every single money.
Truly too much good

Mafia II is a fantastic game with a very strong story and great characters. You play as Vito Scaletta, a young man who returns from war and becomes involved in the mafia world. The story is emotional, interesting, and shows friendship, loyalty, and the dangerous life of crime.

The game has a great atmosphere, especially because of the music, cars, and the beautiful city inspired by the 1940s and 1950s. The graphics still look good and the world feels realistic and alive.

Overall, the whole Mafia series is very engaging and one of the best games ever made and a must-play for people who enjoy story-driven games.
